Verified by two independent methods:
1. Fraction DP (a000568_fast.py) — 672s
2. C batch CRT (a000568_c_batch.c) — ~500s

Performance comparison (Apple Silicon M1, 8 cores):
  Method              | n=50  | n=60  | n=76  | n=80   | n=90   | n=100  | n=120
  --------------------|-------|-------|-------|--------|--------|--------|-------
  Py enum + LCD ★     | 0.02s | 0.05s | 0.9s  | 1.3s   | 3.5s   | 9.1s   | 50s
  C enum + CRT (8c)   | 0.3s  | 0.4s  | 1.1s  | 1.6s   | 5.2s   | 17.9s  | 150s
  Int DP + GCD        |  4.5s |  19s  | 160s  | 276s   | 924s   | ~7200s | N/A
  gmpy2 DP            |  7.9s |  35s  | 229s  | 537s   | 1757s  | 14391s | N/A
  Fraction DP         | 11.5s |  ~85s | 259s  | 672s   | N/A    | N/A    | N/A

  ★ = current fastest. Python direct enumeration of odd partitions with
      LCD-scaled integer accumulation beats all other approaches by 100-1000x.
      Bottleneck: number of odd partitions grows exponentially (~exp(π√(2n/3))).

Best practical approach: Integer DP with periodic GCD reduction (a000568_int_v2.py).
~2x faster than gmpy2 DP for n≥80 by avoiding per-operation GCD overhead.
C batch CRT wins for n≤60 but runs out of memory for n≥90.
Parallel C per-prime CRT is slower than gmpy2 for n≥60 (too many primes needed).
